title: Effect of metformin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes: a randomized placebo-controlled trial
abstract: Background: Metformin is a first-line therapy for type 2 diabetes. We evaluated cardiovascular outcomes over 5 years. Methods: We randomized 4,800 adults with type 2 diabetes to metformin 1500mg/day or placebo. Primary endpoint was a composite of myocardial infarction, stroke, or all-cause mortality. Results: Metformin reduced the primary endpoint by 18% (HR 0.82, 95% CI 0.72-0.93, p=0.003). Conclusions: Metformin reduces cardiovascular events compared to placebo in adults with type 2 diabetes.

title: Long-term cardiovascular safety of metformin in adults with type 2 diabetes: a double-blind randomized trial
abstract: In this multicenter double-blind randomized controlled trial, 3,200 adults with type 2 diabetes received metformin or placebo for 7 years. Cardiovascular mortality was 11.2% in metformin group vs 14.8% in placebo group (p=0.001). Stroke rates did not differ. No safety concerns emerged.

title: Metformin effects on glucose levels in adolescents with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes
abstract: We enrolled 450 adolescents aged 12-17 with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes in a randomized trial of metformin versus placebo. HbA1c was reduced by 0.8% at 26 weeks. No cardiovascular events were observed in this pediatric cohort.

title: Metformin in pre-diabetes: a placebo-controlled study of progression to type 2 diabetes
abstract: Adults with impaired fasting glucose (prediabetes) were randomized to metformin or placebo for 3 years. Progression to type 2 diabetes was reduced by 31%. Cardiovascular outcomes were not assessed.

title: Cohort analysis of metformin use and mortality in patients with type 2 diabetes
abstract: Retrospective cohort study of 22,000 type 2 diabetes patients comparing metformin users to non-users. After propensity matching, metformin was associated with 24% lower all-cause mortality. Observational design with risk of residual confounding.

title: Effects of metformin on insulin resistance in obese mice
abstract: Diet-induced obese C57BL/6 mice were treated with metformin 200mg/kg/day. Insulin sensitivity improved and HbA1c equivalent markers fell. Translational implications for human cardiovascular disease are discussed.

title: Editorial: Metformin and the heart — time to reconsider?
abstract: This editorial summarizes recent debate on whether metformin reduces cardiovascular events. We argue for further trials but provide no original data.

title: Randomized trial of metformin plus sitagliptin versus placebo in adults with type 2 diabetes for cardiovascular outcomes
abstract: This RCT randomized 2,100 adults with T2DM to combination metformin+sitagliptin or placebo over 4 years. Primary endpoint: composite of MI, stroke, CV death. Combination therapy reduced events by 22% (HR 0.78, 95% CI 0.66-0.92).

title: Metformin monotherapy versus placebo for cardiovascular prevention in type 2 diabetes: 10-year follow-up
abstract: Randomized double-blind trial in 2,800 adults with type 2 diabetes. Metformin 2000mg/day vs placebo. After 10 years, MI rate was 6.4% vs 9.1% (HR 0.69), all-cause mortality 12.1% vs 15.8%. Stroke incidence similar.

title: Metformin pharmacokinetics in healthy volunteers
abstract: Single-dose pharmacokinetic study in 40 healthy adult volunteers. Cmax and AUC measurements provided. No clinical outcomes assessed.

title: Effect of metformin on bone density in postmenopausal women with type 2 diabetes: a randomized trial
abstract: Postmenopausal women with type 2 diabetes (n=380) randomized to metformin or placebo for 3 years. Primary outcome: lumbar bone mineral density. Cardiovascular outcomes not analyzed.

title: Metformin vs placebo for cardiovascular events in adults with type 2 diabetes and prior MI: a randomized controlled trial
abstract: Adults with T2DM and prior myocardial infarction (n=1,650) were randomized to metformin or placebo for 4 years. Composite endpoint of recurrent MI, stroke, CV death occurred in 14% vs 19% (HR 0.71, 95% CI 0.58-0.86, p=0.002). Conclusion: metformin reduces recurrent cardiovascular events in this high-risk population.
